title: Expected counts in chi-squared tests with two-way tables
description: Practice calculating expected counts for a chi-square test for independence or homogeneity using a two-way table.
topic_path: Expected counts in chi-squared tests with two-way tables

assessment_items.question_content:
Naina obtained a random sample of $225$ American high school students and collected their responses to the following questions: “What is your gender?” and “What is your favorite season of the year?” Here are the results:

Favorite season | Male | Female | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Spring | $13$ | $\boxed{15}$ | $28$
Summer | $44$ | $27$ | $71$
Fall | $32$ | $49$ | $81$
Winter | $27$ | $18$ | $45$
Total | $116$ | $109$ | $225$

Naina wants to perform a $\chi^2$ test of independence between gender and favorite season in this population.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to female students whose favorite season is spring?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

| Male | Female | Total
- | - | - | -
**Spring** | $13$ | $\boxed{15}$ | $\boxed{\blueD{28}}$
**Summer** | $44$ | $27$ | $71$
**Fall** | $32$ | $49$ | $81$
**Winter** | $27$ | $18$ | $45$
**Total** | $116$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{109}}$ | $\boxed{\greenD{225}}$

In this sample, $\blueD{28}$ of $\greenD{225}$ students chose spring as their favorite season. If gender and favorite season are independent, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\purpleD{109}$ female students.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to female students whose favorite season is spring is:

$\left( \dfrac{\blueD{28}}{\greenD{225}} \right) \cdot \purpleD{109}\approx13.56$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

| Male | Female | Total
- | - | - | -
**Spring** | $13$ | $\boxed{15}$ | $\boxed{28}$
**Summer** | $44$ | $27$ | $71$
**Fall** | $32$ | $49$ | $81$
**Winter** | $27$ | $18$ | $45$
**Total** | $116$ | $\boxed{109}$ | $\boxed{225}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{spring, female}}&=\dfrac{28 \cdot 109}{225} \\\\
&\approx 13.56
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for female students whose favorite season is spring is about $13.56$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A certain car model comes in four different colors (black, white, blue, and silver) and can either have automatic or manual transmission.

The company that makes the car took a random sample of $384$ cars that were sold and checked their color and transmission. Here are the results:

Transmission | Automatic | Manual | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Black| $57$ | $41$ | $98$ 
White | $64$ | $\boxed{32}$ | $96$
Blue | $38$ | $85$ | $123$
Silver | $21$ | $46$ | $67$
Total | $180$ | $204$ | $384$

The company wants to perform a $\chi^2$ test of independence between color and transmission.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to a white car with manual transmission?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

Transmission | Automatic | Manual | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Black| $57$ | $41$ | $98$ 
White | $64$ | $\boxed{32}$ | $\boxed{\blueD{96}}$
Blue | $38$ | $85$ | $123$
Silver | $21$ | $46$ | $67$
Total | $180$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{204}}$ | $\boxed{\greenD{384}}$

In this sample, $\blueD{96}$ of $\greenD{384}$ cars are white. If color and transmission are independent, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\purpleD{204}$ manual cars.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to a white car with manual transmission is:

$\left( \dfrac{\blueD{96}}{\greenD{384}} \right) \cdot \purpleD{204}=51$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

Transmission | Automatic | Manual | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Black| $57$ | $41$ | $98$ 
White | $64$ | $\boxed{32}$ | $\boxed{96}$
Blue | $38$ | $85$ | $123$
Silver | $21$ | $46$ | $67$
Total | $180$ | $\boxed{204}$ | $\boxed{384}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{white,manual}}&=\dfrac{96\cdot 204}{384} \\\\
&=51
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to a white car with manual transmission is $51$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rashad is a hotel manager. He surveyed a random sample of $120$ guests and asked them which floor their room was and about their level of satisfaction. Here are the results:

Hotel floor | Satisfied | Neutral | Not satisfied | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
$1$-$3$| $20$ | $11$ | $8$ | $39$
$4$-$6$ | $24$ | $13$ | $6$ | $43$
$7$-$9$ | $\boxed{23}$ | $10$ | $5$ | $38$
Total | $67$ | $34$ | $19$ | $120$

Rashad wants to perform a $\chi^2$ test of independence between floor and satisfaction.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to satisfied guests that stayed in floors $7$ to $9$?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

Hotel floor | Satisfied | Neutral | Not satisfied | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
$1$-$3$| $20$ | $11$ | $8$ | $39$
$4$-$6$ | $24$ | $13$ | $6$ | $43$
$7$-$9$ | $\boxed{23}$ | $10$ | $5$ | $\boxed{\blueD{38}}$
Total | $\boxed{\purpleD{67}}$ | $34$ | $19$ | $\boxed{\greenD{120}}$

In this sample, $\purpleD{67}$ of $\greenD{120}$ guests were satisfied. If floor and satisfaction are independent, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\blueD{38}$ guests who stayed in floors $7$ to $9$.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to a satisfied guest that stayed in floors $7$ to $9$ is:

$\left( \dfrac{\purpleD{67}}{\greenD{120}} \right) \cdot \blueD{38}\approx 21.22$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

Hotel floor | Satisfied | Neutral | Not satisfied | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
$1$-$3$| $20$ | $11$ | $8$ | $39$
$4$-$6$ | $24$ | $13$ | $6$ | $43$
$7$-$9$ | $\boxed{23}$ | $10$ | $5$ | $\boxed{38}$
Total | $\boxed{67}$ | $34$ | $19$ | $\boxed{120}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{7-9, satisfied}}&=\dfrac{38\cdot 67}{120} \\\\
&\approx 21.22
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to satisfied guests that stayed in floors $7$ to $9$ is about $21.22$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Judson collected information about the name length and the population of a random sample of $296$ American cities. Here are the results:

Name length | Less than $150{,}000$| Between $150{,}000$ and $250{,}000$  | More than $250{,}000$ | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Less than $8$ characters| $30$ | $29$ | $\boxed{41}$ | $100$
$8$ to $10$ characters | $43$ | $41$ | $33$ | $117$
More than $10$ characters | $26$ | $28$ | $25$ | $79$
Total | $99$ | $98$ | $99$ | $296$

Judson wants to perform a $\chi^2$ test of independence between name length and population.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to cities with more than $250{,}000$ residents whose name has less than $8$ characters?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

Name length | Less than $150{,}000$| Between $150{,}000$ and $250{,}000$  | More than $250{,}000$ | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Less than $8$ characters| $30$ | $29$ | $\boxed{41}$ | $\boxed{\blueD{100}}$
$8$ to $10$ characters | $43$ | $41$ | $33$ | $117$
More than $10$ characters | $26$ | $28$ | $25$ | $79$
Total | $99$ | $98$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{99}}$ | $\boxed{\greenD{296}}$

In this sample, $\blueD{100}$ of $\greenD{296}$ have less than $8$ characters in their name. If name length and population are independent, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\purpleD{99}$ cities with more than $250{,}000$ residents.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to cities with more than $250{,}000$ residents whose name has less than $8$ characters is:

$\left( \dfrac{\blueD{100}}{\greenD{296}} \right) \cdot \purpleD{99}\approx 33.45$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

Name length | Less than $150{,}000$| Between $150{,}000$ and $250{,}000$  | More than $250{,}000$ | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Less than $8$ characters| $30$ | $29$ | $\boxed{41}$ | $\boxed{100}$
$8$ to $10$ characters | $43$ | $41$ | $33$ | $117$
More than $10$ characters | $26$ | $28$ | $25$ | $79$
Total | $99$ | $98$ | $\boxed{99}$ | $\boxed{296}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{less than 8,more than 250{,}000}}&=\dfrac{100\cdot 99}{296} \\\\
&\approx 33.45
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to cities with more than $250{,}000$ residents whose name has less than $8$ characters is about $33.45$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Liv collected information about the length and width of a random sample of $48$ petals of iris flowers. Here are the results:

 | Width is less than $2\text{ cm}$| Width is more than $2\text{ cm}$ | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Length is less than $5.2\text{ cm}$| $14$ | $3$ | $17$
Length is $5.2\text{ cm}$ to $5.7\text{ cm}$ | $4$ | $11$ | $15$
Length is more than $5.7\text{ cm}$ | $7$ | $\boxed 9$ | $16$
Total | $25$ | $23$ | $48$

Liv wants to perform a $\chi^2$ test of independence between petal length and width.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to petals whose length is more than $5.7\text{ cm}$ and whose width is more than $2\text{ cm}$?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

 | Width is less than $2\text{ cm}$| Width is more than $2\text{ cm}$ | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Length is less than $5.2\text{ cm}$| $14$ | $3$ | $17$
Length is $5.2\text{ cm}$ to $5.7\text{ cm}$ | $4$ | $11$ | $15$
Length is more than $5.7\text{ cm}$ | $7$ | $\boxed 9$ | $\boxed{\blueD{16}}$
Total | $25$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{23}}$ | $\boxed{\greenD{48}}$

In this sample, $\blueD{16}$ of $\greenD{48}$ petals are more than $5.7\text{ cm}$ long. If petal length and width are independent, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\purpleD{23}$ petals that are more than $2\text{ cm}$ wide.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to petals whose length is more than $5.7\text{ cm}$ and whose width is more than $2\text{ cm}$ is:

$\left( \dfrac{\blueD{16}}{\greenD{48}} \right) \cdot \purpleD{23}\approx 7.67$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

 | Width is less than $2\text{ cm}$| Width is more than $2\text{ cm}$ | Total
- | -: | -: | -:
Length is less than $5.2\text{ cm}$| $14$ | $3$ | $17$
Length is $5.2\text{ cm}$ to $5.7\text{ cm}$ | $4$ | $11$ | $15$
Length is more than $5.7\text{ cm}$ | $7$ | $\boxed 9$ | $\boxed{16}$
Total | $25$ | $\boxed{23}$ | $\boxed{48}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{length > 5.7,width > 2}}&=\dfrac{16\cdot 23}{48} \\\\
&\approx 7.67
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to petals whose length is more than $5.7\text{ cm}$ and whose width is more than $2\text{ cm}$ is about $7.67$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tate works at an ice cream stand. Every customer can choose one of two toppings—sprinkles or chocolate chips. He recorded the age group and the chosen topping for a random sample of $120$ customers. Here are the results:

Age group | Sprinkles | Chocolate chips | Neither | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Children| $27$ | $38$ | $7$ | $72$
Adults | $\boxed{13}$ | $22$ | $13$ | $48$
Total | $40$ | $60$ | $20$ | $120$

Tate wants to perform a $\chi^2$ test of independence between age and topping.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to adults that chose sprinkles?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

Age group | Sprinkles | Chocolate chips | Neither | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Children| $27$ | $38$ | $7$ | $72$
Adults | $\boxed{13}$ | $22$ | $13$ | $\boxed{\blueD{48}}$
Total | $\boxed{\purpleD{40}}$ | $60$ | $20$ | $\boxed{\greenD{120}}$

In this sample, $\purpleD{40}$ of $\greenD{120}$ customers chose sprinkles. If age and topping are independent, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\blueD{48}$ adult customers.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to an adult that chose sprinkles is:

$\left( \dfrac{\purpleD{40}}{\greenD{120}} \right) \cdot \blueD{48}=16$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

Age group | Sprinkles | Chocolate chips | Neither | Total
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Children| $27$ | $38$ | $7$ | $72$
Adults | $\boxed{13}$ | $22$ | $13$ | $\boxed{48}$
Total | $\boxed{40}$ | $60$ | $20$ | $\boxed{120}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{adult,sprinkles}}&=\dfrac{48\cdot 40}{120} \\\\
&=16
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to adults that chose sprinkles is $16$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A random sample of internet subscribers from the west coast of the United States  was asked if they were satisfied with their internet speeds. A separate random sample of adults from the east coast was asked the same question. Here are the results:

Satisfied? | East | West | Total
:- | -: | -: | -:
Yes | $\boxed{14}$ | $34$ | $48$
No | $55$ | $81$ | $136$
Neither | $11$ | $5$ | $16$
Total | $80$ | $120$ | $200$

A market researcher wants to perform a $\chi^2$ test of homogeneity on these results.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to east coast subscribers who responded "yes"?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

Satisfied? | East | West | Total
:- | -: | -: | -:
Yes | $\boxed{14}$ | $34$ | $\boxed{\blueD{48}}$
No | $55$ | $81$ | $136$
Neither | $11$ | $5$ | $16$
Total | $\boxed{\purpleD{80}}$ | $120$ | $\boxed{\greenD{200}}$

In this sample, $\blueD{48}$ of $\greenD{200}$ subscribers responded "yes." If satisfaction is homogenous between each coast, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\purpleD{80}$ subscribers in the east coast sample.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to east coast subscribers who responded "yes" is:

$\left( \dfrac{\blueD{48}}{\greenD{200}} \right) \cdot \purpleD{80}=19.2$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

Satisfied? | East | West | Total
:- | -: | -: | -:
Yes | $\boxed{14}$ | $34$ | $\boxed{48}$
No | $55$ | $81$ | $136$
Neither | $11$ | $5$ | $16$
Total | $\boxed{80}$ | $120$ | $\boxed{200}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{yes, east}}&=\dfrac{48 \cdot 80}{200} \\\\
&= 19.2
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to east coast subscribers who responded "yes" is $19.2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mabel made a change to the website she runs, and she wonders how it affected the way people navigate to the website—from a web search, from social media, or directly by entering the website’s address.

She took a random sample of $170$ visits before she made the change, and then she took a random sample of $170$ visits after she made the change. Here are the results:

Mode of navigation | Before | After | Total
:- | -: | -: | -:
Search | $78$ | $95$ | $173$
Social media | $\boxed{57}$ | $71$ | $128$
Direct | $35$ | $4$ | $39$
Total | $170$ | $170$ | $340$

Mabel wants to perform a $\chi^2$ test of homogeneity on these results.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to navigation from social media before Mabel made the change?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

Mode of navigation | Before | After | Total
:- | -: | -: | -:
Search | $78$ | $95$ | $173$
Social media | $\boxed{57}$ | $71$ | $\boxed{\blueD{128}}$
Direct | $35$ | $4$ | $39$
Total | $\boxed{\purpleD{170}}$ | $170$ | $\boxed{\greenD{340}}$

In this sample, $\blueD{128}$ of $\greenD{340}$ visits were from social media. If the mode of navigation is homogenous before and after Mabel's change, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\purpleD{170}$ visits that were sampled before the change.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to navigation from social media before Mabel made the change is:

$\left( \dfrac{\blueD{128}}{\greenD{340}} \right) \cdot \purpleD{170}=64$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

Mode of navigation | Before | After | Total
:- | -: | -: | -:
Search | $78$ | $95$ | $173$
Social media | $\boxed{57}$ | $71$ | $\boxed{128}$
Direct | $35$ | $4$ | $39$
Total | $\boxed{170}$ | $170$ | $\boxed{340}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{social,before}}&=\dfrac{128\cdot 170}{340} \\\\
&= 64
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to navigation from social media before Mabel made the change is $64$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Margot surveyed a random sample of $180$ people from the United States about their favorite sports to watch. Then she sent separate, similar, survey to a random sample of $180$ people from the United Kingdom. Here are the results:

Favorite sport to watch | United States | United Kingdom | Total
:- | -: | -: | -:
Basketball | $60$ | $51$ | $111$
Football | $67$ | $14$ | $81$
Soccer | $28$ | $86$ | $114$
Tennis | $25$ | $\boxed{29}$ | $54$
Total | $180$ | $180$ | $360$

Margot wants to perform a $\chi^2$ test of homogeneity on these results.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to people from the United Kingdom whose favorite sports to watch is tennis?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

Favorite sports to watch | United States | United Kingdom | Total
:- | -: | -: | -:
Basketball | $60$ | $51$ | $111$
Football | $67$ | $14$ | $81$
Soccer | $28$ | $86$ | $114$
Tennis | $25$ | $\boxed{29}$ | $\boxed{\blueD{54}}$
Total | $180$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{180}}$ | $\boxed{\greenD{360}}$

In this sample, $\blueD{54}$ of $\greenD{360}$ people prefer to watch tennis. If the favorite sports to watch is homogenous for the United States and the United Kingdom, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\purpleD{180}$ people in the United Kingdom sample.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to people from the United Kingdom whose favorite sports to watch is tennis is:

$\left( \dfrac{\blueD{54}}{\greenD{360}} \right) \cdot \purpleD{180}=27$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

Favorite sports to watch | United States | United Kingdom | Total
:- | -: | -: | -:
Basketball | $60$ | $51$ | $111$
Football | $67$ | $14$ | $81$
Soccer | $28$ | $86$ | $114$
Tennis | $25$ | $\boxed{29}$ | $\boxed{54}$
Total | $180$ | $\boxed{180}$ | $\boxed{360}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{tennis,United Kingdom}}&=\dfrac{54\cdot 180}{360} \\\\
&= 27
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to people from the United Kingdom whose favorite sports to watch is tennis is $27$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Maud and Billy wanted to know if there's a difference between the modes of transportation used by men and women.

One morning, they stood at the entrance to a big office building and surveyed people about the mode of transportation they used to get to the office. Maud surveyed a random sample of $110$ women and Billy surveyed a random sample of $110$ men. Here are the results:

Gender | Car | Bus | Bicycle | Walk | Total
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
Men | $54$ | $31$ | $12$ | $13$ | $110$
Women | $41$ | $38$ | $16$ | $\boxed{15}$ | $110$
Total | $95$ | $69$ | $28$ | $28$ | $220$

They want to perform a $\chi^2$ test of homogeneity on these results.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to women who walk?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

Gender | Car | Bus | Bicycle | Walk | Total
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
Men | $54$ | $31$ | $12$ | $13$ | $110$
Women | $41$ | $38$ | $16$ | $\boxed{15}$ | $\boxed{\blueD{110}}$
Total | $95$ | $69$ | $28$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{28}}$ | $\boxed{\greenD{220}}$

In this sample, $\purpleD{28}$ of $\greenD{220}$ people walked to the office. If the mode of transportation is homogenous for men and women, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\blueD{110}$ women.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to women who walk:

$\left( \dfrac{\purpleD{28}}{\greenD{220}} \right) \cdot \blueD{110}=14$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

Gender | Car | Bus | Bicycle | Walk | Total
:- | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
Men | $54$ | $31$ | $12$ | $13$ | $110$
Women | $41$ | $38$ | $16$ | $\boxed{15}$ | $\boxed{110}$
Total | $95$ | $69$ | $28$ | $\boxed{28}$ | $\boxed{220}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{women,walk}}&=\dfrac{110\cdot 28}{220} \\\\
&= 14
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to women who walk is $14$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Edison, Xiu, and Amia are childhood friends who go to different high schools. They were wondering if there's a difference between their schools in the number of friends people have on social media.

They each surveyed a random sample of students from their schools, asking for the number of friends they have on social media. Here are the results:

School | Less than $100$ friends | $100$ to $300$ friends | More than $300$ friends | Total
:- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Edison's school | $8$ | $61$ | $\boxed{21}$ | $90$
Xiu's school | $9$ | $54$ | $25$ | $88$
Amia's school | $7$ | $58$ | $19$ | $84$
Total | $24$ | $173$ | $65$ | $262$

They want to perform a $\chi^2$ test of homogeneity on these results.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to students from Edison's school that have more than $300$ friends?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

School | Less than $100$ friends | $100$ to $300$ friends | More than $300$ friends | Total
:- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Edison's school | $8$ | $61$ | $\boxed{21}$ | $\boxed{\blueD{90}}$
Xiu's school | $9$ | $54$ | $25$ | $88$
Amia's school | $7$ | $58$ | $19$ | $84$
Total | $24$ | $173$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{65}}$ | $\boxed{\greenD{262}}$

In this sample, $\purpleD{65}$ of $\greenD{262}$ students have more than $300$ friends. If number of friends is homogenous for all schools, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\blueD{90}$ students from Edison's school.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to students from Edison's school that have more than $300$ friends:

$\left( \dfrac{\purpleD{65}}{\greenD{262}} \right) \cdot \blueD{90}\approx 22.33$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

School | Less than $100$ friends | $100$ to $300$ friends | More than $300$ friends | Total
:- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Edison's school | $8$ | $61$ | $\boxed{21}$ | $\boxed{90}$
Xiu's school | $9$ | $54$ | $25$ | $88$
Amia's school | $7$ | $58$ | $19$ | $84$
Total | $24$ | $173$ | $\boxed{65}$ | $\boxed{262}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{Edison,>300}}&=\dfrac{90\cdot 65}{262} \\\\
&\approx 22.33
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to students from Edison's school that have more than $300$ friends is about $22.33$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A zoo has separate habitats for its three types of bears: brown, black, and grizzly bears. Each habitat offers three areas for the bears to spend their time: an outdoor area, an indoor area, and a pool.

Gisele is the zookeeper who cares for the brown bear. She takes a random sample of $10$ moments each day for a week and records which area the brown bear is at each of those moments. Bryan and Adele use a similar strategy and obtain separate samples of $70$ observations for the black and grizzly bears, respectively. Here are their results:

Bear| Outdoor | Indoor | Pool | Total
:- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Brown | $23$ | $27$ | $20$ | $70$
Black | $15$ | $18$ | $37$ | $70$
Grizzly | $32$ | $12$ | $\boxed{26}$ | $70$
Total | $70$ | $57$ | $83$ | $210$

They want to perform a $\chi^2$ test for homogeneity to judge if the bears are spending their time differently in their habitats.

**What is the expected count for the cell corresponding to the grizzly bear being by the pool?**  
*You may round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

Expected: [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Intuition

Bear| Outdoor | Indoor | Pool | Total
:- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Brown | $23$ | $27$ | $20$ | $70$
Black | $15$ | $18$ | $37$ | $70$
Grizzly | $32$ | $12$ | $\boxed{26}$ | $\boxed{\blueD{70}}$
Total | $70$ | $57$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{83}}$ | $\boxed{\greenD{210}}$

In this sample, in $\purpleD{83}$ of $\greenD{210}$ observations the bear was by the pool. If the area to spend time is homogenous for all types of bear, then we'd expect to see the same ratio reflected among the $\blueD{70}$ grizzly bear observations.

###Calculation

The expected count for the cell corresponding to the grizzly bear being by the pool:

$\left( \dfrac{\purpleD{83}}{\greenD{210}} \right) \cdot \blueD{70}\approx 27.67$

###In general

We can generalize this strategy for calculating the expected count for a particular cell:

$\text{expected }=\dfrac{\text{row total}\times\text{column total}}{\text{table total}}$

Bear| Outdoor | Indoor | Pool | Total
:- | -: | -: | -: | -:
Brown | $23$ | $27$ | $20$ | $70$
Black | $15$ | $18$ | $37$ | $70$
Grizzly | $32$ | $12$ | $\boxed{26}$ | $\boxed{70}$
Total | $70$ | $57$ | $\boxed{83}$ | $\boxed{210}$

$\begin{align}
\text{expected}_{\text{grizzly,pool}}&=\dfrac{70\cdot 83}{210} \\\\
&\approx 27.67
\end{align}$

###Answer

The expected count for the cell corresponding to the grizzly bear being by the pool is about $27.67$.
